sections.title: Definite Articles (the) — ο, η, το
sections.explanation:
Greek definite articles agree with the noun in gender (masculine/feminine/neuter), number (singular/plural), and case (nominative/accusative/genitive). The basic nominative forms are:

Singular: ο (masc.) · η (fem.) · το (neut.)
Plural:   οι (masc.) · οι (fem.) · τα (neut.)

Accusative singular: τον (masc.) · την (fem.) · το (neut.)
Accusative plural:   τους (masc.) · τις (fem.) · τα (neut.)

Genitive singular: του (masc.) · της (fem.) · του (neut.)
Genitive plural:   των (masc.) · των (fem.) · των (neut.)

Greek uses the definite article far more than English — with names of people, languages, abstract concepts, and in many set phrases where English omits 'the'.
sections.tip: Masculine and feminine plurals are both 'οι' in the nominative — the same form! Neuter plural is always 'τα'. Genitive plural is always 'των' for all genders.

examples.en: I see the father. (masculine accusative singular)

exercises.prompt: What is the correct definite article for 'άντρας' (man, masculine)?
exercises.explanation: Masculine nouns take 'ο' in the nominative singular: ο άντρας (the man).

exercises.prompt: What is the correct definite article for 'γυναίκα' (woman, feminine)?
exercises.explanation: Feminine nouns take 'η' in the nominative singular: η γυναίκα (the woman).

exercises.prompt: Which article goes with 'παιδιά' (children, neuter plural)?
exercises.explanation: Neuter plural nouns take 'τα': τα παιδιά (the children). Neuter plurals always use τα.

sections.title: Indefinite Articles (a / an) — ένας, μία, ένα
sections.explanation:
Greek indefinite articles (meaning 'a' or 'an') only exist in the singular — there is no plural indefinite article in Greek. The forms are:

Nominative: ένας (masc.) · μία / μια (fem.) · ένα (neut.)
Accusative: έναν / ένα (masc.) · μία(ν) / μια(ν) (fem.) · ένα (neut.)

Note: 'ένας' is also the number 'one'. In the accusative, the masculine 'έναν' (with final -ν) is used before vowels or certain consonants, and 'ένα' before others. The feminine 'μίαν/μιαν' retains -ν in formal/written registers. There is no plural equivalent — Greek simply omits the article: 'βλέπω παιδιά' (I see children).
sections.tip: Only three forms to learn: ένας (m), μία (f), ένα (n). No plural! 'ένας' doubles as the number 'one' — context tells them apart.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'a dog' in Greek? (σκύλος — masculine)
exercises.explanation: Masculine nouns use 'ένας': ένας σκύλος (a dog).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'a city' in Greek? (πόλη — feminine)
exercises.explanation: Feminine nouns use 'μία': μία πόλη (a city).

exercises.prompt: Which is correct for 'a book'? (βιβλίο — neuter)
exercises.explanation: Neuter nouns use 'ένα': ένα βιβλίο (a book).

sections.title: When to Use (or Omit) Articles in Greek
sections.explanation:
Greek uses the definite article in many situations where English omits 'the':

1. WITH PEOPLE'S NAMES: Greeks almost always use the article before first names and surnames.
   Ο Γιώργης είναι εδώ. = George is here.
   Η Μαρία τηλεφώνησε. = Maria called.

2. WITH LANGUAGES: Languages always take the definite article.
   Μαθαίνω τα ελληνικά. = I am learning Greek.
   Μιλά τα αγγλικά καλά. = She speaks English well.

3. WITH ABSTRACT NOUNS: Abstract and general concepts use the article.
   Η αγάπη είναι σημαντική. = Love is important.

4. WITH COUNTRIES, CITIES (often): Many place names take an article.
   Η Ελλάδα είναι όμορφη. = Greece is beautiful.
   Η Αθήνα = Athens.

5. OMIT with professions after 'to be' (informal), and in some set expressions.

The indefinite article is omitted when speaking generally in the plural: Βλέπω σκύλους. = I see dogs (some dogs).
sections.tip: When in doubt, use the definite article in Greek — it appears where English has nothing. The biggest surprises are names of people and languages, which always take the article.

examples.en: Nikos lives in Athens. (article before personal name)
I am learning Greek. (article before language)
Greece is very beautiful. (article before country)
Love is strength. (article before abstract noun)
I see dogs in the park. (no article in plural for 'some dogs')
Maria speaks French. (article before both name and language)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Kostas is my friend' in Greek? (Κώστας — male name)
exercises.explanation: Greek uses the definite article before personal names: Ο Κώστας (not just Κώστας).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am learning Greek' in Greek?
exercises.explanation: Languages take the definite article in Greek: 'τα ελληνικά'. Language names are not capitalised in Greek.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'Greece is beautiful'?
exercises.explanation: Country names in Greek take the definite article: Η Ελλάδα (Greece). Omitting it sounds unnatural.
